coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodot doped in polymer film: Plasmophore enhancement, catalytic amination and white-light generation
coredata.dc:creator: Abbas, Farogh
Kumar, Sagar
Pal, Surja Kanta
Panda, Debashis
coredata.dc:description: A broader application of carbon nanodot (CD) gets limited due to its feeble emissivity at the red end of the emission spectrum. In this work, we have adopted two-pronged strategies to augment the photoluminescence properties of CD. First, the polymer has been used to avoid aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). And the further augmentation of the photoluminescence properties of CD by the surface plasmon of silver nanoparticle (Ag-NP). This has been demonstarted by stepwise emission enhancement of emitters present in CD covering the entire visible spectrum. Polymer composite films have been prepared at varying concentrations of Ag-NP for direct observation of metallic nanoparticle-enhanced photoluminescence (MNEP). Such observation has been attributed to the strong bonding interactions of thiol-groups and other functional groups present at the surface of carbon nanodot with the silver nanoparticles. Unprecedentedly, Ag-NP augments nearly 25% of the emission intensity of CD. Interestingly, nanodot is found to exhibit Ag-NP concentration-dependent switching of photoluminescence in composite films and aqueous solutions as well. We show here CD-doped polymer film has the potential to generate white light through LED-down conversion and can effectively catalyze the reductive amination of resazurin and 2,4-dinitrophenol in real-time with relatively high reaction rates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodot
Silver nanoparticle
Polymer
Radiative decay engineering
Dip-catalyst
White-light production

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Solvothermal reaction of citric acid with ammonium thiocyanate in DMF
LED down-conversion with a nanodot/polymer composite film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors greatly acknowledge Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), Govt. of India (Project No.: 01(2954)/18/EMR-II) for the financial support. S.K.P. thanks RGIPT, for fellowship. Authors express sincere gratitude to Prof. Anindya Datta, IIT Bombay for availing the facility of time-resolved emission measurements. Authors are thankful to Souradip Dasgupta and Shuvankar Mandal for their help in experimentations. Authors acknowledge Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility (SAIF), IIT Bombay for Electron Microscopic Characterizations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: [A] Schematic representation of synthesis of luminescent carbon dot. [B] Absorption spectra of carbon dot (blue solid line); Synchronous Emission spectra of carbon dot, Δλexc = 20 nm. [C] Excitation-dependent emission spectra of synthesized carbon dot. [D] TEM image of carbon nanodot. Inset: Particle Size distribution. [E] Dried PVA/PVP film embedded with carbon dot in ambient light, [F] the film under UV excitation (365 nm).

[A] Emission spectra of CD embedded in 1% PVA/PVP film recorded at three different concentrations of Ag-NP at λexc = 370 nm (solid line), 440 nm (dashed line) & 560 nm (dotted line). [B] Dependence of emission intensity on Ag-NP concentration, at λems = 450 nm (λexc = 370 nm), 532 nm (λexc = 440 nm), & 628 nm (λexc = 560 nm), [C] Effect of Ag-NP on emission decays of CD doped in polymer film, at λems = 635 nm, λexc = 440 nm, [D] Variation of average emission lifetime on Ag-NP concentration, at λems = 480 nm, 530 nm, & 635 nm for excitation, λexc = 440 nm. [E] Stress–strain curves for CD embedded PVA/PVP film loaded with three different concentration of Ag-NP. Only PVA/PVP film (dark grey), film embedded with CD (blue line), Embedded film with- 0.5 pM Ag-NP (green line), 5.1 pM Ag-NP (red line), and 20.5 pM Ag-NP (dark red line).

[A] FTIR spectra of CD in dried state. [B] Absorption spectra (normalized) of carbon dot in presence of Ag-NP in aqueous solution. [C] Effect of Ag-NP Concentration on the emission spectra of carbon dot at three excitations, λexc = 370 nm, 450 nm, & 550 nm. [D] Dependence of emission intensity on Ag-NP concentration, at λems = 455 nm (λexc = 370 nm), 520 nm (λexc = 450 nm), & 590 nm (λexc = 550 nm).

Simplified Jablonksii Diagram for Nanodot in the absence [A] and presence [B] of Silver nanoparticle at 0.5 pM concentration. [C] Schematic representation of the interaction of nanodot in PVA/PVP film with Ag-NP.

[A] Schematic representation of generation of white light using LED chip. [B-C] The arrangement of CD embedded PVA/PVP films on the LED chip at ambient light, [D-E] White light generation after turning on the GaN UV LED at 30 V.

[A] Schematic representation of heterogeneous catalytic reaction using carbon dot as catalyst for reduction of resorufin. [B] Time-dependent absorption spectra of catalytic reaction of resazurin. Inset- color change observed in the cuvette before & after the reaction, [C] Pictorial representation of carbon dot as catalyst for reduction of 2,4-dinitrophenol. [D] Time-dependent absorption spectra of catalytic reaction of 2,4-dinitrophenol
Radiative and nonradiative rate constants for CD in PVA/PVP nanocomposite film and aqueous medium.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodot doped in polymer film: Plasmophore enhancement, catalytic amination and white-light generation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Sciences and Humanities, Rajiv Gandhi Institute of Petroleum Technology, An Institution of National Importance, Jais 229304, Uttar Pradesh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Rajiv Gandhi Institute of Petroleum Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Sciences and Humanities, Rajiv Gandhi Institute of Petroleum Technology, An Institution of National Importance, Jais 229304, Uttar Pradesh, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A broader application of carbon nanodot (CD) gets limited due to its feeble emissivity at the red end of the emission spectrum. In this work, we have adopted two-pronged strategies to augment the photoluminescence properties of CD. First, the polymer has been used to avoid aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). And the further augmentation of the photoluminescence properties of CD by the surface plasmon of silver nanoparticle (Ag-NP). This has been demonstarted by stepwise emission enhancement of emitters present in CD covering the entire visible spectrum. Polymer composite films have been prepared at varying concentrations of Ag-NP for direct observation of metallic nanoparticle-enhanced photoluminescence (MNEP). Such observation has been attributed to the strong bonding interactions of thiol-groups and other functional groups present at the surface of carbon nanodot with the silver nanoparticles. Unprecedentedly, Ag-NP augments nearly 25% of the emission intensity of CD. Interestingly, nanodot is found to exhibit Ag-NP concentration-dependent switching of photoluminescence in composite films and aqueous solutions as well. We show here CD-doped polymer film has the potential to generate white light through LED-down conversion and can effectively catalyze the reductive amination of resazurin and 2,4-dinitrophenol in real-time with relatively high reaction rates.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodot (CD) is one of the most exciting class of carbon allotrope and hence being investigated with great enthusiasm due to the one-pot synthetic method, tunable emission properties, easy processability, and compatibility for various applications- optoelectronics, bio-imaging, and energy conversion & storage [1–6]. The quest for a multifunctional nanodot has led to the elemental doping of nanodots, primarily using nitrogen and sulfur. This class of nanodots has been studied in recent times with renewed fervor by using various molecular dopants as precursors [7–10]. The most contentious subject in nanodot research is the observation of excitation-dependent photoluminescence and gradual decrease of emission intensity with increased excitation wavelength [11,12]. Among the proposed hypotheses of excitation-dependent emission, the one suggesting the formation of different kinds of fluorophores under hydrothermal conditions is gaining importance [13–15]. However, the feeble emission of CD at the red end of the emission spectrum has been attributed to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), thus, remains a bottleneck for the development of luminescence-based devices in biological and chemical sciences. Hence, there is a demand to augment the emission intensity, in particular, at the red end of the emission of carbon nanodot [16–19]. To address this directly, we have adopted two strategies- first, modulation of water content, and further, the addition of foreign substances for enhancing photoluminescence intensity of carbon nanodot.

We have, first, impregnated CD in polymer matrix for the regulation of the water molecules in the vicinity of nanodot. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/ polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) polymeric network has been chosen as a model polymer system due to the presence of extensive hydroxyl groups that can offer a polar-protic environment like water [14,20,21]. Moreover, the free space available in the polymer networks allows the entrapment of water molecules inside this three-dimensional structure. Foreign substances can easily get loaded in the network at different ratios, and further, modulate the swelling/deswelling properties of polymer by shifting the equilibrium of free and bound water content [20]. Such type of modulation is not possible in bulk water, thus, polymers are an effective system to quantify the effect of solvent-induced quenching of photoluminescence properties of carbon nanodot. Barman et al. have observed that the photoluminescence property of blue-emissive CD in a polymer is its concentration-dependent [22]. At lower CD’s concentration in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix, emission intensity initially increases and then tends to decrease at its high concentration. CD doped polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film has been demonstrated for its efficacy in the conversion of UV (365 nm)-to-blue (440 nm) light. Metallic nanoparticles, such as Ag, Au which are primarily known for their roles in quenching the photoluminescence of probes in solution studies [23–25], contrarily have also been found to augment the emission properties by several folds, namely in a method known as metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) wherein the near-field interaction of fluorophores with metallic nanoparticles leads to substantial fluorescence enhancements [26–29]. An increased rate of the intrinsic emission of the fluorophore has resulted from the effects of the creation of both - an intense excitation field around the metal nanoparticle, and “plasmophore” in which plasmons in the metallic nanoparticles are strongly coupled with the fluorophore [30,31]. The plasmophore system radiates into the far-field with increased emission intensity. To quantify the role of silver nanoparticles (Ag-NP) in the augmentation of emission properties of carbon nanodot, the pre-synthesized nanoparticle is impregnated by filler mixing method in polymer matrix along with nanodot.

In general, white light is produced by using a combination of three emissive species- red, green and blue (RGB) [7,32–34]. Nanodot/polymer composite film has been employed for light-emitting diode (LED) down-conversion [35]. We report here a one-pot methodology for the generation of white-light through LED down-conversion and the potential use of synthesized carbon nanodot in the catalytic transformation of organic compounds, primarily the reductive amination. Photocatalytic activities of nanodots have been investigated with a renewed interest for exploring a new horizon towards metal-free organic transformation [36–39]; however, there exists limited reports on the catalytic (homogeneous) role of nanodot for organic transformation. Primarily, it has been employed either as a homogenous catalyst for covalent aminocatalytic transformations or a composite with metal oxide for the oxidative conversion of alcohols, aromatic rings, and others [40–43]. We offer nanodot/ polymer composite film-based dip-catalyst for the reductive amination reactions.

In this work, citric acid and ammonium thiocyanate have been used as precursors for the synthesis of doped nanodot. The choice of ammonium thiocyanate is made for finding a suitable alternative to thiourea, a commonly used molecular dopant used for the synthesis of doped nanodot. Solvothermal in DMF has been done for the creation of a color gamut. We have studied the emission properties of the as-synthesized nanodot embedded in polyvinyl alcohol/polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVA/PVP) film. Silver nanoparticles with varying concentrations in the picomolar range have been impregnated in a polymer matrix to augment the emissive properties of carbon nanodot. Steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopic studies have been carried out to pinpoint the role-reversal of silver nanoparticle from augmentation to quenching of emission of nanodot. We have demonstrated here the CD-doped polymer film for the reduction reactions of resazurin and 2,4-dinitrophenol.

Citric acid (22585, >99%), Silver Nitrate (27464, >99%) and Resazurin (189900050) from Fisher Scientific, and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (28973) from SRL are purchased. Ammonium thiocyanate (20036 K05, 99%), polyvinyl alcohol (39791 K05), and silica (25121 K05) are obtained from SDFCL company. Sodium borohydride (QH1Q610462, >95%) from Merk, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) (44080) from Alfa-Aesar, 2,4 Dinitrophenol (DNP) (31515, 99%) and hydrazine hydrate (7803–57-8, >80%) from Molychem were procured. The product code number and purity of the above chemicals used in the experiments are included and these are used without any purification. Solvents such as dimethyl formamide (DMF), ethyl acetate (EA), Acetone, Petroleum ether are received from Finar Chemicals. The purity of solvents used in the experiments was >99.5%. Water from ELGA LAB Water-purifier (18.2 mΩ) is used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvothermal reaction of citric acid with ammonium thiocyanate in DMF
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots were prepared by following a method developed in our laboratory [14]. Specifically, 0.4 g of citric acid and 0.4 g of ammonium thiocyanate were dissolved in 10 mL DMF and mixed thoroughly for carrying out solvothermal reaction. The transparent solution was then transferred into a Teflon-coated stainless steel autoclave (purchased from M/S Shilpa Enterprises, Nagpur, India) at 160 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the color of the solution obtained from solvothermal reaction was changed to blood red from colorless. The colored solution was passed through a Whatmann 42 filter paper (2.5-µm pore size) to remove large particles. Thereafter, the as-synthesized product was added into 200 mL of ethyl acetate–petroleum ether mixture (50:50) and thoroughly mixed for 20 min. The insoluble part has been extracted by adding 2 mL of water. The solution had been used thereafter for spectroscopic studies and fabrication of polymer composite film.

Solvent evaporation method was employed to prepare the composite PVA/PVP film. In brief, 2.0 g of PVA and 2.0 g of PVP were dissolved in 40 mL of water, mixed using a magnetic stirrer at 70 °C for 4 h. The PVA/PVP solution was then cooled down to 50 °C, subsequently, the 40 mL solution was divided into four parts, 10 mL of each, and then 1 mL of freshly prepared and purified solution of CD and varying concentrations of Ag-NP which was synthesized by NaBH4 reduction method [44] (Fig. S1), were added separately to it and mixed for 30 min. After that, the solution mixture was poured into a polyethylene petri dish and kept under room temperature for 7 days to cast the composite films. The dried films were used for optical and mechanical characterization studies. In order to rationalize the effect of silver nanoparticle on emissive properties of carbon dot in polymer matrix, composite films were impregnated with three different concentration of silver nanoparticle- low as 0.5 pM (PicoMolar), 5.1 pM and relatively high as 20.5 pM. For direct comparison with solution studies, the silver nanoparticle’s concentration was also been kept in that range- 2.6 to 25.7 pM.

Briefly, 0.2 g of PVDF and 0.1 g of PVP were mixed with 10 mL of DMF and it was sonicated at 35 °C for 30 min. Then the solution was transferred to a hot plate stirrer at 70 °C and mixed for 30 min, to which 0.5 mL of freshly prepared CD and further mixed. The content of the beaker was then heated to 110 °C for 30 min and then allowed to cool to 70 °C and then poured into a petri dish and maintained at 70 °C on a hot plate to slowly evaporate the DMF. This led to the formation of dry nanodot/PVDF film which was used as a catalytic strip for catalytic studies. 2 mL of 200 mM N2H4 was taken in both quartz cuvettes. Then 50 µL of purified resazurin was added in one cuvette and mixed thoroughly. For reduction of 2,4-dinitrophenol, 10 mL of 200 µM of its solution was mixed with 10 mL of 50 mM NaBH4 solution. From the mixture, 2 mL solution was taken for catalytic transformation. Then the nanodot/PVDF film composite was dipped carefully in the solution and spectra were taken at 1 min time intervals.

The steady-state absorption spectra were recorded using a LAB India UV–Vis 3200 spectrophotometer. Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to record the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two FT-IR spectrometer was employed to record the FT-IR spectra of the vacuum dried samples. The samples were recorded using “attenuated total reflectance” (ATR) mode. The PIKE MIRacleTM single reflection horizontal ATR accessory was used for recording the FT-IR spectra. Time-resolved emission measurements were performed using Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) system, from IBH, UK. Excitation sources used in this experiment were- pulsed diode lasers of 375 nm and 440 nm with instrument response function (IRF) near about 300 ps. Emissions from products at various emission wavelengths were collected at right angles to the direction of the excitation beam, at magic angle polarization to nullify the rotational anisotropy effect. The decay traces for each of the excitation were analyzed with IBH DAS v6.2 data analysis. Using the iterative re-convolution method, the data were fitted to the sum of multi-exponential functions as shown below:
I
t
= I
( 0 )
∑ i
A i
e x p
-
t
τ i
I ( t )
and
I ( 0 )
are the emission intensities at time t and zero after excitation by the source.
A i
is the amplitude of the i-th component.

The samples were imaged by a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL, JEM 2100F) mounted with field emission gun FEG TEM at 200 kV accelerating voltage. The samples for TEM were prepared on amorphous carbon films supported on a carbon-coated copper grids. The average values were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Mechanical properties of the dried nanocomposites were measured by using an H5KL UTM Tinius Olsen Benchtop Testing Machine. The dried films were subjected to stress–strain measurements at ∼30 °C using a 5 kN load cell and at a crosshead speed of 5 mm/min. Tensile strength was measured in MPa unit which represents the maximum force (N) per unit cross-sectional area (mm2) required to break the sample. Elongation at break (E) is defined as the percent ratio of the length of the nanocomposite at the maximum force to the original length. The samples of width ∼6.5 mm and thickness ∼0.08 mm were used during measurements and the data represented here was an average of three specimens. LED down conversion studies were carried out by using a 45 mil GaN chip of 30 W power with luminous flux 160 LM, 32–34 V Forward voltage, and wavelength length 395–400 nm.

The reaction of citric acid and a molecular dopant, ammonium thiocyanate under solvothermal conditions using DMF has yielded a deep red solution suggesting the formation of chromophoric units (Fig. 1 A). The absorption spectra of nanodot in aqueous solution show the presence of multiple peaks, clearly identifiable at 336 nm, 557 nm, and shoulder peaks at 455 nm and 517 nm respectively (Fig. 1B). Transitions lying in the high energy region are generally assigned to π–π*, whereas n–π* transitions tend to appear in the low energy region respectively [45].

The chromophoric units present in nanodot are expected to possess varied light absorption cross-sections and interactions with solvent, water. It is imperative to settle the issue on the emitters attached with doped nanodots. This has been performed conveniently through synchronous fluorescence experiments. In this method, excitation and emission wavelengths are changed simultaneously with a constant offset, such that the experiment is performed along the diagonal of the excitation-emission matrix, which passes through the maximum number of peaks and troughs. The resulting spectra have a greater number of narrower peaks compared with that obtained in conventional excitation spectroscopy, allowing discrimination between luminescent molecules that have broad and overlapping spectra [46–48]. The synchronous luminescence spectrum of nanodot exhibits four peaks ranging from blue to green region- 390 nm, 460 nm, 501 nm, and 561 nm, respectively, thus corroborating the presence of multiple, at least three emitters as observed in absorption measurements (Fig. 1B). The steady-state emission spectra of products recorded at different excitation wavelengths for the synthesized nanodot are shown in Fig. 1C. Expectedly, a strong excitation-dependent emission and an emerging state with emissivity at 585 nm have also been noticed for nanodot.

Considering the presence of multiple emitters in nanodot and expecting their varied interaction with water, we have used polymers, such as PVA/PVP primarily for its offering of a water-like environment due to the presence of a large number of hydroxyl groups [20,49]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) has been used here to characterize the synthesized carbon nanodot in vacuum conditions. The TEM image recorded at high concentration shows particle formation (Fig. 1D). The average diameter of the nanodot is measured to be 4.50 ± 0.88 nm (Inset, Fig. 1D). We have also observed a lattice pattern with 0.20 nm spacing (Fig S2). PVA/PVP film impregnated with synthesized nanodot remains transparent and reddish in color (Fig. 1E). Surprisingly, it illuminates as a white light emitter under irradiation at 365 nm under a UV chamber (Fig. 1F).

Observation of white light emission from nanodot impregnated polymer film has established that solvent-induced quenching a dominant factor for green and red emitters present in nanodot and thus, paves the way for further tinkering of photoluminescence properties of nanodot. Metallic nanoparticle (plasmonic)-enhanced fluorescence experiments were generally carried out using organic dyes, and these fluorophores are allowed to come close contact with the nanoparticle surface by using biotin-avidin chemistry [50,51].

Thus, we have undertaken nanoparticle-enhanced photoluminescence studies to ascertain the enhancement of nanodots’ photoluminescence by the presence of near-field surface plasmons. Moreover, we have used composite polymer film instead, containing both plasmonic silver nanoparticles and nanodot film to find an alternative to commonly used nanoparticle coated glass surfaces for MEF experiments. Metal nanoparticles are widely reported for quenching the luminescence properties of molecular probes. Hence, we have varied the concentration of nanoparticles in the polymer matrix to point of inflection.

The absorption spectra of polymer films at low concentrations of the silver nanoparticle are expectedly dominated by carbon nanodot (Fig. S3). Whereas, at the concentration of 20.5 pM of nanoparticle, the contribution of unique surface plasmon of silver nanoparticle tends to show its signature in the spectrum. Considering the presence of multi-emitting species, we have excited at three wavelengths- 370 nm, 440 nm, and 560 nm respectively to cover the entire range of absorption spectrum of nanodot. The steady-state photoluminescence of nanodot in nanocomposite film has been presented in Fig. 2 A. The emission spectrum recorded at 370 nm excitation shows the presence of multiple shoulder peaks. The photoluminescence intensities in the green (for excitation at 440 nm) and red region (for excitation at 560 nm) of emission spectrum of CD have been augmented significantly in the polymer film. Unprecedentedly, at the lowest concentration, 0.5 pM of silver nanoparticle, we have noticed the significant enhancement of photoluminescence of nanodot, and thereafter, quenching at an increased concentration of plasmonic nanoparticle. To understand the Ag-NP concentration-dependent switching of photoluminescence properties we have constructed a plot of emission intensities of nanodot at three wavelengths- 450 nm, 532 nm, and 628 nm, which are collected using excitations at 370 nm, 440 nm, and 560 nm respectively against the concentration of Ag-NP (Fig. 2B). It reveals that a maximum ∼25% enhancement of photoluminescence has been observed for nanodot at very low concentration, i.e. 0.5 pM of Ag-NP, whereas nearly 85% quenching of photoluminescence of nanodot at 20.5 pM Ag-NP concentration has been noticed. The dependence of emission intensities at 450 nm and 532 nm over Ag-NP concentration are almost identical.

The temporal properties of CD/Ag-NP/polymer composite film have been characterized by picosecond time-resolved fluorescence measurements. The photoluminescence lifetimes of the nanodot in the films are measured at three different emission wavelengths- 480 nm, 530 nm, and 635 nm, and excitation wavelength is kept at 440 nm (Fig. S4). The decay traces at 635 nm emission wavelength for composite films have been presented in Fig. 2C. Interestingly, it also captures the Ag-NP concentration-dependent switching behavior, specifically increased photoluminescence lifetime at low concentration and thereafter its decrease at higher concentration of silver nanoparticle. The decay traces are well fitted with tri-exponential and corresponding parameters are tabulated in Table S1. Since the decays are multi-exponential, therefore, then it is pertinent to use an average decay time. The variation of average photoluminescence lifetime with the concentration of Ag-NP has been presented in Fig. 2D. Average photoluminescence lifetimes of nanodot in composite films, except low concentration one, remains nearly unaltered with Ag-NP concentrations. The emission lifetime at the low concentration of Ag-NP is strikingly different, significantly higher at 635 nm than in comparison to that at 480 nm, and 530 nm respectively. This could be due to the specific interaction of emitters contributing to emission at 635 nm with nanocomposite surface. In general, the emission lifetime of emitter on metal surface in MEF study is found to get quenched dramatically. But in polymer matrix, the emission lifetime in the presence and absence of plasmonic nanoparticles remains unchanged, thus offers uncompromising avenues for fluorescence lifetime-related studies.

Analysis of decay traces of CD/Ag-NP/polymer composite film at 440 nm excitation reveals the presence of three components: a very short component with a lifetime (τ1) of about 0.5 ns and a long component ∼7.5 ns for emission wavelengths across all concentration of silver nanoparticles. Interestingly, another component shows emission wavelength dependence, with a lifetime (τ1) of about ∼ 2.0 ns for 480 nm, ∼2.7 ns for 530 nm, and ∼3.8 ns for 635 nm emission wavelengths. For low concentration, 0.5 pM of silver nanoparticle, the long component for emission at 635 nm is markedly higher, about ∼11.5 ns with a relatively high amplitude and that has contributed significantly to the average photoluminescence lifetime for that film. Moreover, there is a slight decrease in average photoluminescence lifetime for nanodot at 0.5 pM concentration of Ag-NP in the polymer film.

It highlights that the nanodot in the composite polymer film prepared at a low concentration of Ag-NP is experiencing a different environment than other films. Such changes should be reflected in the material properties of the composite films. In order to understand that the mechanical properties of the dried composite films are measured (Fig. 2E). As expected from the stress–strain curve that the tensile properties of the composite films are strikingly different than the base material. The addition of nanodot has resulted in a remarkable increase in elongation at break. The composite film prepared at low concentration, 0.5 pM of Ag-NP is found to be relatively stiffer and possess the highest Young modulus, 214 Nm−2 in comparison to that of other films. The Young moduli for other films are- 177 Nm−2 for a film containing only CD, 119 Nm−2, and 71 Nm−2 for films containing 5.1 pM Ag-NP and 20.5 pM Ag-NP. Moreover, at 20.5 pM of Ag-NP, the material properties switch to a relatively more ductile nature.

Corroborative Evidence: FT-IR spectroscopy is first performed for the identification of functional groups present in the synthesized nanodot (Fig. 3 A and Fig S5). From 3700 to 2700 cm−1, the stretching vibrations of NH, OH, COOH and CH contribute to the broad IR band. Interestingly, the characteristic peak of the thiol group (SH) has appeared at 2510 cm−1 [52]. The stretching vibration at 2058 cm−1 is assigned to SCN bonding [53]. The strong FTIR bands at 1708 cm−1, 1662 cm−1, and 1618 cm−1 belong to CO, CN, and CC vibrations respectively. At 1435 cm−1, a strong CN stretching has also been observed [54]. Thus, the presence of the thiol, amine carboxylic groups at the surface of nanodot has strongly contributed to its covalent bonding with the silver nanoparticle.

It is well-reported that SH group is highly reactive and can even react with metallic nanoparticles in an aqueous solution as well. This has led to study of the interaction of synthesized nanodot with silver nanoparticle in the aqueous medium. The concentration ranges of Ag-NP used for solution studies and composite polymer films have been kept nearly the same for direct rationalization of its interaction with nanodot in two drastically different environments. With the increase in the concentration of silver nanoparticle, the characteristic plasmon band, near 400 nm tends to appear prominently in the absorption spectra (Fig. 3B). The emission spectrum of nanodot in presence of Ag-NP has been recorded at three different excitations, 370 nm, 450 nm, and 550 nm respectively (Fig. 3C). The strong interaction of nanodot with silver nanoparticle can be noticed here as well. Specifically, blue and red emitters present in nanodot are showing stronger interaction with nanoparticle than green-emissive ones. In solution studies also, we have observed concentration-dependent switching properties of silver nanoparticle. Further, to quantify the augmentation of photoluminescence properties we have constructed a plot of emission intensities of nanodot at three wavelengths- 455 nm, 520 nm, and 590 nm collected using excitations at 370 nm, 450 nm, and 550 nm respectively against the concentration of Ag-NP (Fig. 3D). It reveals that a maximum enhancement ∼30% (at 455 nm) and ∼40% (at 590 nm) of photoluminescence has been observed for nanodot at lower concentrations, 2.6–5.1 pM of plasmonic nanoparticle, whereas nearly 23% quenching of photoluminescence (at 455 nm) of nanodot at 25.7 pM concentration of Ag-NP has been quantified. The dependence of emission intensities over Ag-NP concentration is almost identical. The temporal properties of CD in presence of silver nanoparticle in aqueous solutions have been characterized by picosecond time-resolved fluorescence measurements as well. The photoluminescence lifetimes of the nanodot are measured at three different emission wavelengths- 450 nm, 530 nm, and 600 nm, and excitation wavelength is kept at 370 nm. The decay traces are well fitted with tri-exponential and the average photoluminescence lifetimes remain unaltered with silver nanoparticle concentrations (Fig S6). Nonradiative rates (kNR) of CD in presence of Ag-NP remain almost unaltered, whereas, radiative rate (kNR) is relatively higher at the lower concentration of Ag-NP and whereas it dramatically gets lowered at the higher concentration of Ag-NP (Table 1 ).

It now brings us to rationalize the photoluminescence dynamics of CD in composite polymer films (Fig. 4 ). First, the augmentation of photoluminescence of nanodot in the polymer matrix in comparison to solution studies suggests that the solvent, water molecules play a vital role in quenching the emission of green and red emitters present in nanodot. Further augmentation of photoluminescence of nanodot in the film containing 0.5 pM Ag-NP is the convolution of – (i) stiffness of the material and (ii) surface-plasmon enhanced emission. Loss of free-water in polymer matrix causes increased stiffness of the material, thus resulted in a decrease of nonradiative rate (kʹnr) [55,56]. It is well-known that a decrease of non-radiative rate enhances both- photoluminescence intensity and lifetime, assuming that radiative rate remains unaltered (Fig. 4A) [57].

Whereas, in metal-enhanced fluorescence, the increased radiative rate which is due to the creation of plasmophore will be reflected with an increase of photoluminescence intensity but a decrease of photoluminescence lifetime, assuming the nonradiative remains unaltered [58]. The observation of slightly decreased average lifetime suggests that additional radiative-decay rate (km) caused by plasmophore due to the silver nanoparticle has a relatively lesser impact in dictating photoluminescence lifetime of carbon nanodot in the composite film (Fig. 4B). A greater degree of photoluminescence quenching of carbon nanodot at a higher concentration of Ag-NP and unchanged emission lifetime indicates that the static quenching is the dominant factor in these cases. It can be predicted that the surface-specific groups of nanodot interact strongly with silver nanoparticles for static quenching to become operative. The use of ammonium thiocyanate as a precursor in the synthesis has perhaps contributed to the creation of thiol groups at the surface of nanodot (Fig. 4C). Such specific interaction nanodot-nanoparticle should be operative in solution study as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LED down-conversion with a nanodot/polymer composite film
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of blue, green, and red-emitters in the synthesized nanodot offers a unique one-pot strategy to generate white light. Moreover, the emissivity of green and red emitters presents in nanodot get enhanced significantly in the polymer film. Thus, the nanodot /polymer composite film will be an excellent choice for the down-conversion of light-emitting diodes. The Gallium Nitride (GaN) based UV LED is selected as an excitation source for down conversion studies [35]. The CD-doped polymer film is used here by placing them onto a GaN UV LED chip using a spacer and adhesive for a simple prototype of LED down-conversion (Fig. 5 ). The required voltage for LED was provided by using a 30 V DC power source. The nanodot /polymer composite film has the capabilities of LED down conversion that even generates white-light directly (Fig. 5 D-E). With CIE 1931, the chromaticity index is expected to be the same as in polymer film 0.22, 0.25 [14].

Heterogeneous catalysts are preferred over homogenous ones even at the cost of efficiency due to better recovery and reuse. Carbon nanodot is an organo-catalyst and thus free from any metal. However, the bottleneck of using carbon nanodots as catalysts is their greater solubility in organic solvents, making the catalytic separation tedious and expensive. The concept of catalysts embedded in a polymer, known as dip-catalyst is tested for carbon nanodot for the very first time. The use of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) polymer as a framework for nanodot can pave the way to harness the advantages of CD-based catalyst. We tested the ability of the nanodot/PVDF composite film in catalyzing the reduction of resazurin to resorufin and 2,4-dinitrophenol to aminophenol in aqueous solution. The reason for choosing these substrates is that their reaction kinetics and mechanism have been understood in depth [59–62]. The catalytic reactions for resazurin were carried out in the water at room temperature, initiated by dipping nanodot/PVDF composite film into a premixed solution of resazurin and a large excess of N2H4, and monitored by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. Upon use of dip-catalysts, the solution color gradually turns from blue (resazurin) to red (resorufin). The absorption spectrum of the reaction solution shows a decrease of the resazurin absorption at 598 nm and an increase of the resorufin absorption at 568 nm (Fig. 6 A-B) with the progress of time. The calculated rate of the reaction for the catalytic transformation of resazurin is 0.29 s−1 (Fig. S7-8). On the other hand, the catalytic reduction reactions for 2,4-dinitrophenol were carried out by dipping nanodot/PVDF composite film into a premixed solution of DNP and NaBH4. The absorption spectrum of the reaction solution shows over time a decrease of the 2,4-dinitrophenol absorption at 357 nm and an increase of the aminophenol absorption at 428 nm (Fig. 6C-D). The calculated rate of the reduction reaction of 2,4-dinitrophenol is 0.08 s−1 (Fig. S7-8).

Citric acid and ammonium thiocyanate have been used to synthesize doped carbon nanodot. The synthesized nanodots show unique excitation-energy-dependent photoluminescence properties. All three- red, green, and blue (RGB) emitters present in the nanodot with distinct spectral and temporal profiles. In the PVA/PVP polymer film the photoluminescence of CD is significantly enhanced. The excitation-energy-dependent photoluminescence of CD in the composite film is markedly different than that in aqueous solution. The modified metal-enhanced fluorescence method is adopted by incorporating plasmonic Ag-NP in polymer matrix. Ag-NP concentration-dependent photoluminescence switching of carbon nanodot in the composite film is noticed for the very first time. The material properties such as stiffness indicated by the Young modulus of composite films are greatly altered with the concentration of silver nanoparticle. The non-radiative rate is found to be more dominant than Ag NP-induced radiative rate for dictating the photoluminescence properties of carbon nanodot. The thiol-group of carbon nanodot is strongly interacting in both polymer film and aqueous solution. The quenching of photoluminescence was observed for nanodot at higher concentration of Ag-NP in both media- heterogeneous, polymer, and homogenous, neat water. The GaN UV LED down conversion to white light generation has also been achieved by CD/polymer composite film. With CIE 1931, The chromaticity index is 0.22, 0.25. Moreover, CD/PVDF film has been used as a dip-catalyst for catalytic reduction of resazurin to resorufin and 2,4-dinitrophenol to aminophenol in real-time. The catalytic rate constants for the reactions are relatively high, 0.29 s−1 and 0.08 s−1 respectively.

Farogh Abbas: Data Curation, Methodology, Validation, Formal Analysis, Visualization. Sagar Kumar: Data Curation, Methodology, Validation. Surja Kanta Pal: Data Curation, Methodology, Validation, Formal Analysis, Visualization. Debashis Panda: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing, review & editing, Formal Analysis, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), Govt. of India (Project No.: 01(2954)/18/EMR-II) for the financial support. S.K.P. thanks RGIPT, for fellowship. Authors express sincere gratitude to Prof. Anindya Datta, IIT Bombay for availing the facility of time-resolved emission measurements. Authors are thankful to Souradip Dasgupta and Shuvankar Mandal for their help in experimentations. Authors acknowledge Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility (SAIF), IIT Bombay for Electron Microscopic Characterizations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Steady state and time-resolved fluorescence data, FTIR, TEM and others are included. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2021.118001.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
